observations.recommendation:
To enhance asset management and address the noted deficiencies, the following
measures are recommended and Management agreed to:

a. Implement an Asset Management Framework by developing and
institutionalizing an Asset Management Manual that is aligned with the
aforementioned COA Circulars and accounting policies. The manual should
include:
• Roles and Responsibilities: Clearly define the functions of personnel
involved in asset tracking, reconciliation, and disposal, including the GSO
Designate, Accounting Office, and end-user offices.
• Step-by-Step Procedures/ Processes: Establish step-by-step guidelines for
asset acquisition, tagging, issuance, utilization, periodic assessment,
transfer and disposal.
• Forms/Reports: Observe proper documentation of transactions affecting
PPE through the maintenance of required forms/reports.
• Timelines: Specify deadlines for processes, if applicable, report/records
preparation/submission and reconciliations.
• Control Mechanisms: In addition to the mandated annual comprehensive
physical inventory of all assets, enforce a quarterly reconciliation process
between accounting and property records ensuring proper documentation
of discrepancies and implementation of corrective actions.
• Sanctions for Non-Compliance: Outline penalties for violations of the
policies (e.g., delayed preparation/updating of reports, habitual negligence
on the performance of functions) with a graduated system like warnings or
suspensions to ensure adherence and accountability.
b. Reassess manpower complement for the GSO Designate and match it with the
existing operational and regulatory demands and workloads. Also, implement
a capability- building program that includes regular trainings relevant to asset
management and financial reporting.
c. Conduct One-Time Cleansing of PPE Accounts by implementing COA
Circular 2020-006 to resolve concerns related to PPE accounts' accuracy,
existence, and valuation. This one-time cleansing process will involve the
following:
• Conducting complete physical counts.
• Recognizing PPE items found at station.
• Disposing of non-existing/missing PPE items.

To address these observations and mitigate associated risks, we recommended and
Management agreed to take the following actions:

a. Enforce strict liquidation policies:
• Require all accountable officers to settle outstanding cash advances within the
prescribed periods.
• Temporarily suspend additional/new cash advances for officers and
employees with overdue balances until full liquidation is achieved.
b. Strengthen internal controls, monitoring, and reporting:
• Enhance pre-approval review by having the Accounting and Treasurer’s
Offices verify the legality and specificity of cash advance purposes.
• Require notice of the status of previous cash advances from the Municipal
Accountant prior to approving the same.
• Consider public disclosure of liquidation statuses to enable public scrutiny,
enhance transparency and mitigate political influence.
c. Restrict cash advances for elected officials:
• Limit cash advances for elected officials strictly to official travel expenses per
COA Circular 97-002.
d. Implement disciplinary measures:
• Impose sanctions, such as withholding of salary or reprimands, for delayed
liquidations.

STATUS OF SUSPENSIONS, DISALLOWANCES AND CHARGES
There were no notices of audit suspension, disallowance and charge issued by the
audit team during the year under audit nor there were balances then unsettled at the
beginning of the audit year.

We recommended that the management further improve the level of its
compliance with the rules of RA 9184 and the quality of its procurement processes to
ensure transparency and competitiveness in government procurement through adoption
of appropriate measures, as far as practicable, that would reinforce the faithful
observance of the required activities in the conduct of competitive bidding as outlined in
the Revised IRR of RA 9184; and to promptly submit perfected contracts and other
required supporting documents to facilitate timely review and evaluation.

In this regard, we recommended for Management to adopt mechanisms to ensure
adherence and compliance with the guidelines on the grant, utilization, and liquidation
of cash advances, particularly on the following:
a. Require the AO to liquidate previous cash advance for CIF amounting to
₱1,926,516.00 before granting new cash advance.
b. Issue demand letter to AOs for the liquidation of ₱ 10,797,900.41, granted to
officers and for operating expenses.
c. Stop the practice of granting CA to elected officials other than for official
traveling expense, pursuant to COA Circular 97-002.
d. Discontinue the practice of granting cash advances to facilitate transactions
where the observance of the government procurement processes is required.

We recommended for the Management to:
a. Maximize the utilization of the LDRRMF in order to increase the level of
resilience of the Municipality against the occurrence of disasters in keeping
with the provisions of R.A. No. 10121.
b. Enhance the preparation of the LDRRMP by providing specific timelines and
implementing the PPAs within the stated timelines.
c. Improve evaluation and monitoring mechanisms to benchmark and measure
the use and disbursement of the LDRRMF based on the LDRRMP.

b. improve the collection efficiency by
participating in the revenue setting process to

c. enhance its operating practices to further
reinforce controls over its RPT and SET
collections through institutionalizing measures
that would ensure that RPT and SET collections
are promptly remitted by collectors, and
immediately deposited to an AGDB; and
d. enhance the reliability of its financial

recognized in the year it was earned. To
reclassify the prior years’ income and to correct
the current year RPT and SET income accounts,
we also recommended Proposed Adjusting
Journal Entries.

improve, where practicable, the quality of its 20% DF

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and Management agreed that the LGUs of the province further
enhance the quality of its individual LDRRM management processes by taking concrete
actions to address the issues related to manpower, data and information, and
formalization to reinforce the establishment of an enabling level of coordination in a
collective setting and ensure disaster resiliency.

We recommended and the Management, in their future endeavors, agreed to revisit
the guidelines on the provision of funds for programs, projects and activities to
address the COVID-19 situation especially on budget realignment and revision of
MDRRMFIP to ensure that procedural controls are not bypassed.

We also recommended and the management concurred to:
a. review its current practices in relation to the accounting of inventory held for
distribution; and
b. improve the management of relief goods by establishing a more defined and
stronger controls.

We recommended and the Management agreed to enhance its mechanisms on
accounting and property custodianship of its PPE through the following:

a. Conduct a complete and thorough physical inventory-taking and
reconciliation of accounts. Determine whether remaining unrecognized assets
found at the station are owned by the Municipality. Once identified under the
Municipality, appraised such assets and recognized them into their proper
accounts. For items identified as for reclassification, revisit standards and
related issuances on these items for proper accounts to be used;
b. Enhance accountabilities over assets through identifying and updating records
and monitoring particularly the Acknowledgement Receipt for Equipment
which should be renewed every three (3) years;
c. Commence depreciation of immovable properties once they are already in use.
Determine unrecognized prior and current years depreciations of said assets
and reflect in the statement of changes in equity and statement of financial
performance, respectively;
d. Identify all equipment which are primarily used for response and rescue
operations as well as their corresponding costs and accumulated depreciations
and reclassify them to Disaster Response and Rescue Equipment account.
Revisit guidelines provided under COA Circular No. 2012-002 for proper
accounting and reporting of these equipment and other related items included
therein;
e. Identify uninsured assets with more exposure on risk of deterioration or
damage due to fortuitous events. Consider the significance of possible loss
which may occur due to any unexpected and unforeseen events; and
f. Establish a General Services Office as a separate unit with personnel focusing
on the main duties and responsibilities of the office as provided by the Local
Government Code of the Philippines.

We recommended and the Management agreed to improve its current 20% DF
planning process by establishing mechanisms that warrants availability of relevant and
up-to-date data and information; properly functioning and strengthened structures and
arrangements; and adequacy and capacities of manpower complement to advance
coordination, cohesion, inclusion and integration in order to ensure the crafting of
inclusive, responsive and sustainable development initiatives as well as enhance its
eventual delivery.

We recommended and the Management committed to ensure, as far as
practicable, an optimum level of delivery of 20% DF initiatives through institution of
appropriate arrangements and mechanisms that will effectively manage matters
affecting implementation, and enhance aspects relating to periodic monitoring and
communication.

We recommended and the Management concurred to establish necessary
operating modalities that will effectively address concerns affecting the timeliness,
accuracy and reliability of 20% DF reporting in order to reinforce accountability.

We recommended and Management agreed on the following measures to rectify the financial statements and improve practices, viz:
1. Conduct regular accounts reconciliation to facilitate immediate detection of discrepancies and timely preparation of adjusting entries, when warranted, thereby reinforcing the preparation of accurate and reliable reports;
2. Accrue the interest charges of the existing loan contracts to present the correct interest expenses of the borrowings for the year, and reclassify the current portion of long-term loan obligations and present these payables under Current Liabilities category in the Statement of Financial Position; and
3. Strengthen commitment through faithful adherence with the requirements set forth in IPSAS 1 to ensure the fair presentation of financial statements.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the following:
1. Prepare the Bank Reconciliation Statements every month upon receipt of the Bank Statements issued by its authorized depository bank and submit promptly the bank reconciliation statements, including the bank statements and used checks to COA for verification and audit, in accordance with abovementioned rules and regulations;
2. Reconcile the balances per book with that of the balances per bank to determine what caused the discrepancy. Effect the necessary adjustments as may be required to clear the reconciling items, if any, in order to present fairly the account balances in the financial statements; and
3. Make a representations with the depository/servicing banks for the early release of the bank statements and other supporting documents such as bank debit/credit memos, paid checks, etc. or request for a bank snapshot which could be made available immediately. Also, enroll in the “We Access program” of the bank, if any to have an updated access of the transaction.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant exert extra effort to review and identify the nature of Receivables accounts that are long overdue. And, where collectability of any is remote, the agency head may, after exhausting all earnest efforts to collect, request to write-off said account from the COA, with all the documents required under Section 8 of COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

We recommended that the concerned office reimburse the SEF the amount of P265,757.94 for expenses which are chargeable to the general fund, by transferring these expenses as prior years’ adjustments to the general fund; and that the local school board strictly monitor the utilization of SEF, such that only allowable expenses as provided in the DepEd, DBM and DILG Joint Circular are charged against it.

We recommended the following:
1. Require the Municipal Accountant and the GSO/Municipal Treasurer to maintain PPE Ledger Cards and Property Cards, respectively for each category of asset per fund, and conduct periodic reconciliation of their respective records to forestall possible discrepancies. Any discrepancy should be immediately investigated and properly adjusted in the books to fairly present the said account in the financial statements; and
2. Require the Inventory Committee to conduct physical inventory of all assets of the Municipality pursuant to Paragraph 2, Section 124 of the Manual on NGAS, Volume I, and to ensure the completion thereof.

We recommended and the management agreed that all unserviceable properties be returned to the GSO and cancel the ARE to clear the persons accountable. We further recommend the unserviceable PPEs be immediately disposed through public bidding or transfer to avoid further deterioration.

We recommended and the management agreed that a policy or guidelines in the acquisition and distribution of cellphone unit and motorcycle be drafted immediately. In addition, specific guidelines as to the liability of the end users must also be stipulated, in case item is lost or wasted or used in illegal or improper usage.

Instruct the Accountable Officers and employees to strictly observe the fundamental principles in the disbursements as provided for in section 89 of PD 1445 and in Item 5.1.3 of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.
Stop granting of additional cash advances unless previous cash advances are first settled to avoid accumulation thereof.
Enforce full liquidation of cash advances, any unexpended amount shall be refunded in full amount.

We recommend that the management furnish COA with copies of Contracts and Purchase Orders and other pertinent documents such as but not limited to the bidding documents, Notice of Award, Notice to Proceed; etc. relative thereto within five (5) days after its perfection for timely evaluation, review and inspection in compliance with COA Circular 2009-001 and institute measures to efficiently and timely submit the required documents.

Sanggunian Bayan should invite resource persons and allow constituents to attend and observe during regular and special sessions to attain transparency. Post important accomplishments, resolutions and ordinances affecting the lives and properties of the community in conspicuous places such as the Municipal Bulletin Board, Barangays Bulletin Board and even in the Public Market so as to inform and update the constituents.

Submit duly signed contract agreement in compliance per Notice of Suspension Issued. Advertising expenses for broadcast radio program should be clearly defined as to the purpose and intent to avoid personal and/or political activities.

The Municipal Engineer should provide COA not only listings of projects to be implemented on the current year but also those on-going projects funded from prior years’ development fund and including those NGA-funded projects.

The management should post/ display notifications/billboards/signage at the least possible cost, to inform the public on the details of the project implementation. The billboard/signage should include necessary details about the implemented project, such as but not limited to, source of fund, date started, project duration and its target date of completion. However, initials, symbols or graphic representation associated with the leadership of the project proponent or of the local officials should not be included in the billboards/signage.

Inform the COA within ten (10) days after the award of the infrastructure project or before the start of the program/activity that the appropriate project signboards and/or public notices are already posted.
